entry.title: The Wild Womaniya Archetype Field Guide
entry.summary: The Wild Womaniya Archetype Discover the Feeling Your Website Should Create Introduction Many people start building a website by choosing colors. Or fonts. Or themes. Or page layouts. Most of the time, they’re starting in the wrong place. Before you decide what your website should…
entry.content:
The Wild Womaniya Archetype

Discover the Feeling Your Website Should Create

Introduction

Many people start building a website by choosing colors.

Or fonts.

Or themes.

Or page layouts.

Most of the time, they’re starting in the wrong place.

Before you decide what your website should look like, you need to decide what your website should feel like.

A website is not just a collection of pages.

It is an experience.

And experiences are shaped by identity.

This guide will help you discover the feeling, atmosphere, and experience your website should create before you begin designing it.

Who This Is For

This guide is for:

  • creators
  • artists
  • writers
  • coaches
  • healers
  • freelancers
  • small business owners
  • anyone building a personal brand

Who This Is Not For

This guide is not for:

  • people looking for technical design tutorials
  • people searching for branding trends
  • people who want a ready-made template

How This Guide Will Help

By the end of this guide, you will:

  • understand the connection between identity and design
  • identify the feeling your website should create
  • make clearer design decisions
  • avoid copying other people’s brands
  • create a more authentic online presence

Core Observation

Many websites look attractive.

Far fewer feel aligned.

When people struggle with branding, the problem is often not design.

The problem is clarity.

Without clarity, every design decision becomes difficult.

With clarity, many design decisions become obvious.

Main Framework

Step 1: Identify Your Natural Energy

Ask yourself:

When people interact with my work, what do they consistently experience?

Examples:

  • calm
  • clarity
  • inspiration
  • courage
  • curiosity
  • comfort
  • creativity
  • depth

Choose three words.

Not ten.

Three.

Step 2: Define The Visitor Experience

Imagine someone lands on your website.

Complete this sentence:

“When they leave, I want them to feel…”

Examples:

  • encouraged
  • understood
  • energized
  • hopeful
  • reassured
  • inspired

Write down your answer.

Step 3: Look For Alignment

Compare:

What you naturally bring

with

What you want visitors to experience.

Look for overlap.

Example:

If your natural strengths are:

  • calm
  • clarity
  • simplicity

and you want visitors to feel:

  • reassured
  • supported
  • confident

your website should probably feel:

  • spacious
  • welcoming
  • easy to navigate

Not loud. Not chaotic.

Aligned.

Step 4: Translate Feelings Into Design

Examples:

If the desired feeling is:

Calm

Consider:

  • clean layouts
  • generous spacing
  • simple navigation

Creativity

Consider:

  • visual variety
  • expressive imagery
  • playful language

Trust

Consider:

  • clarity
  • consistency
  • straightforward communication

The feeling guides the design.

Not the other way around.

Step 5: Build Around The Experience

Every decision should support the experience.

Ask:

Does this:

  • image
  • headline
  • color
  • page
  • section

support the feeling I want people to have?

If not, remove or replace it.

Field Observations

Observation #1

Many people choose aesthetics before choosing experiences.

Observation #2

Copying someone else’s website rarely produces authentic results.

Observation #3

Clarity often creates stronger brands than originality.

Observation #4

People remember feelings more than design details.

Observation #5

Aligned websites are usually easier to maintain because decisions become simpler.

Practical Exercise

Complete these sentences:

My work helps people feel:

When visitors leave my website, I want them to feel:

Now compare both lists.

Circle any words that appear in both.

Those words are clues.

Quick Start Version

Today:

  1. Choose three words that describe your work.
  2. Choose three words that describe the experience you want visitors to have.
  3. Look for overlap.
  4. Make one website change that strengthens that feeling.

Keep it simple.

Checklist

Before changing your website, ask:

  • What feeling am I creating?
  • Is that feeling intentional?
  • Does this design choice support it?
  • Does this content support it?
  • Would removing something improve clarity?

If you’re unsure, simplify.

Next Step

Review your homepage.

Not your entire website.

Just your homepage.

Ask:

“What feeling does this page create?”

If the answer is unclear, start there.

Final Reflection

A strong website does not begin with colors.

It does not begin with fonts.

It does not begin with templates.

It begins with clarity.

When you know how you want people to feel, everything else becomes easier to build.

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative identity of Rakshitha Prakash—an outlet that reflects her exploration of creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts and restarts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

What part landed hardest?

What are you struggling with right now?

If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya

entry.title: The Confidence Without Performance Field Guide
entry.summary: Confidence Without Performance How to Talk About Your Skills Without Bragging Introduction Many capable people struggle to talk about what they do. Not because they lack skill. Not because they lack experience. But because they worry about sounding arrogant. So they downplay their abilities. They…
entry.content:
Confidence Without Performance

How to Talk About Your Skills Without Bragging

Introduction

Many capable people struggle to talk about what they do.

Not because they lack skill.

Not because they lack experience.

But because they worry about sounding arrogant.

So they downplay their abilities.

They soften their achievements.

They hide the very things that make them useful.

The result is a strange situation:

The people most worried about bragging are often the people least likely to do it.

This guide offers a simpler approach.

You do not need to perform confidence.

You only need to tell the truth.

Who This Is For

This guide is for:

  • creators
  • freelancers
  • artists
  • consultants
  • coaches
  • small business owners
  • anyone uncomfortable talking about their abilities

Who This Is Not For

This guide is not for:

  • people looking for persuasion tactics
  • people trying to appear more successful than they are
  • people seeking aggressive marketing techniques

How This Guide Will Help

By the end of this guide, you will:

  • understand the difference between confidence and arrogance
  • communicate your skills more clearly
  • stop minimizing your abilities
  • describe your work honestly
  • make it easier for others to understand how you can help

Core Observation

Facts are not bragging.

If you can build a website in a day, saying so is not arrogance.

If you have written twenty guides, saying so is not arrogance.

If you helped someone solve a problem, saying so is not arrogance.

The problem is not confidence.

The problem is often exaggeration.

Confidence is truth spoken clearly.

Arrogance is superiority performed loudly.

They are not the same thing.

Main Framework

Step 1: Separate Facts From Opinions

Facts can be observed.

Examples:

  • I published ten guides.
  • I built this website.
  • I completed this project.
  • I worked with these clients.

Opinions cannot be proven.

Examples:

  • I am the best.
  • Nobody does this better.
  • My work is superior.

Stay close to facts.

Facts are enough.

Step 2: Describe What You Do

Many people hide behind vague language.

Instead of:

“I help people transform their lives.”

Try:

“I help people create simple publishing systems.”

Instead of:

“I create powerful digital experiences.”

Try:

“I build simple websites that help creators share their work.”

Specific language builds trust.

Step 3: Let Evidence Carry The Weight

Show:

  • examples
  • projects
  • guides
  • case studies
  • results

Evidence is quieter than self-promotion.

And usually more convincing.

Step 4: Stop Shrinking Useful Information

Sometimes what feels like bragging is actually useful context.

If you solved a problem:

Say so.

If you learned something:

Share it.

If you built something:

Show it.

People cannot benefit from work they never discover.

Step 5: Focus On Service

Ask:

“Does sharing this help someone?”

If the answer is yes, share it.

When the goal is usefulness, confidence becomes easier.

Field Observations

Observation #1

Many people underestimate themselves more often than they overestimate themselves.

Observation #2

The people who worry most about bragging are usually not the problem.

Observation #3

Specificity feels more trustworthy than hype.

Observation #4

Evidence creates confidence faster than affirmations.

Observation #5

People are often relieved when someone clearly explains what they can do.

Practical Exercise

Write down three things you do well.

Now remove all exaggerated language.

Write only the facts.

Example:

Instead of:

“I create transformative online experiences.”

Try:

“I build simple websites for creators.”

Read both versions.

Notice which feels clearer.

Quick Start Version

Today:

  1. Choose one skill you regularly use.
  2. Describe it using only facts.
  3. Remove vague language.
  4. Share that description somewhere.

Keep it simple.

Keep it true.

Checklist

Before describing your work, ask:

  • Is this true?
  • Can I demonstrate it?
  • Is it specific?
  • Is it useful information?
  • Am I explaining or performing?

If it is true and useful, say it.

Next Step

Review your:

  • website
  • social media bio
  • service descriptions
  • introduction

Replace one vague statement with a clear factual statement.

Small improvements create clarity.

Final Reflection

You do not need to become louder.

You do not need to become more impressive.

You do not need to perform confidence.

You only need to tell the truth clearly enough that other people can see it.

That is often more than enough.

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative identity of Rakshitha Prakash—an outlet that reflects her exploration of creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts and restarts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

What part landed hardest?

What are you struggling with right now?

If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya

entry.title: The Calm Technology Field Guide
entry.summary: Calm Technology: WordPress or Publii? How to Choose a Website Builder That Matches the Way You Work Introduction Most website builder comparisons focus on features. They compare: Those things matter. But they are not the most important question. The most important question is: “Which system…
entry.content:
Calm Technology: WordPress or Publii?

How to Choose a Website Builder That Matches the Way You Work

Introduction

Most website builder comparisons focus on features.

They compare:

  • plugins
  • themes
  • integrations
  • pricing
  • technical specifications

Those things matter.

But they are not the most important question.

The most important question is:

“Which system am I actually likely to use consistently?”

A website builder that matches the way you naturally work will usually outperform a more powerful system that feels exhausting.

This guide will help you choose based on your goals, habits, and working style.

Who This Is For

This guide is for:

  • creators
  • writers
  • artists
  • freelancers
  • solopreneurs
  • beginners building their first website
  • people deciding between WordPress and Publii

Who This Is Not For

This guide is not for:

  • large ecommerce businesses
  • agencies managing dozens of client sites
  • highly technical developers looking for advanced customization

How This Guide Will Help

By the end of this guide, you will:

  • understand the strengths of both systems
  • identify which one fits your working style
  • avoid unnecessary complexity
  • make a decision with confidence

Core Observation

The best website builder is not the most powerful one.

It is the one you will actually use.

Many creators choose systems based on what they might need someday.

A better approach is choosing based on what you need today.

Future problems can be solved later.

Current problems need solving now.

Main Framework

Step 1: Decide What You Are Building

Ask:

“What am I building right now?”

Examples:

A writer may need:

  • homepage
  • blog
  • about page
  • contact page

An artist may need:

  • portfolio
  • gallery
  • about page

A freelancer may need:

  • service pages
  • portfolio
  • contact page

Most people need less than they think.

Step 2: Understand WordPress

WordPress is a city.

It contains:

  • thousands of themes
  • thousands of plugins
  • endless customization

Strengths:

  • highly flexible
  • huge community
  • extensive documentation
  • can grow with your business

Challenges:

  • updates
  • plugin management
  • maintenance
  • occasional troubleshooting

WordPress is ideal for people who want flexibility and growth.

Step 3: Understand Publii

Publii is a cottage.

You build your website on your computer.

When finished, you publish it.

Strengths:

  • simple
  • lightweight
  • fast
  • secure
  • low maintenance

Challenges:

  • fewer integrations
  • smaller ecosystem
  • less flexibility

Publii is ideal for people who value simplicity and focus.

Step 4: Match The Tool To The Creator

Choose WordPress if:

  • you enjoy customization
  • you expect significant growth
  • you want maximum flexibility
  • you are comfortable learning systems

Choose Publii if:

  • you prefer simplicity
  • you want fewer moving parts
  • you value calm technology
  • you want to spend more time creating than maintaining

Step 5: Make A Decision

Do not spend months researching.

Choose.

Build.

Learn from experience.

A real website teaches more than endless comparisons.

Field Observations

Observation #1

Many creators overestimate how much technology they need.

Observation #2

A simple website published today beats a complex website planned for six months.

Observation #3

Most visitors never know what platform your website uses.

Observation #4

People care about clarity and usefulness more than technical sophistication.

Observation #5

Changing platforms later is possible.

Waiting forever is not useful.

Practical Exercise

Write down:

  1. What am I building?
  2. How much maintenance am I willing to handle?
  3. Do I enjoy customization or simplicity?
  4. How much time do I want to spend managing technology?

Now answer honestly.

Your answers will usually point clearly toward one platform.

Quick Start Version

Choose:

WordPress

If you want:

  • flexibility
  • growth
  • customization

Choose:

Publii

If you want:

  • simplicity
  • speed
  • low maintenance

Then spend the next hour building instead of researching.

Checklist

Before choosing:

  • Do I understand what I’m building?
  • Am I choosing for today or for imaginary future problems?
  • How much maintenance can I realistically handle?
  • Do I value flexibility or simplicity?
  • Am I ready to start?

If yes, make the decision.

Next Step

Choose one platform.

Install it.

Create:

  • a homepage
  • an about page
  • a contact page

Do not optimize.

Do not compare.

Build.

Final Reflection

Technology should support your work.

Not become your work.

Choose the system that helps you spend more time creating and less time maintaining.

That is usually the right choice.

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative identity of Rakshitha Prakash—an outlet that reflects her exploration of creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts and restarts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

What part landed hardest?

What are you struggling with right now?

If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya

entry.title: The "Your Website Is Not a Brochure" Field Guide
entry.summary: Your Website Is Not a Brochure Build a Digital Home, Not a Digital Flyer Introduction Many websites fail for a simple reason. They answer the wrong question. Most website owners ask: “What information should I put on my website?” A better question is: “How should…
entry.content:
Your Website Is Not a Brochure

Build a Digital Home, Not a Digital Flyer

Introduction

Many websites fail for a simple reason.

They answer the wrong question.

Most website owners ask:

“What information should I put on my website?”

A better question is:

“How should people feel when they arrive?”

People rarely remember every detail on a website.

They remember whether they felt:

  • welcomed
  • understood
  • confused
  • overwhelmed
  • inspired
  • reassured

A website is not a brochure.

It is an experience.

And experiences are what people remember.

Who This Is For

This guide is for:

  • creators
  • artists
  • writers
  • coaches
  • freelancers
  • service providers
  • small business owners
  • anyone building a personal brand

Who This Is Not For

This guide is not for:

  • people who only need a temporary information page
  • businesses with no interest in building relationships
  • people looking for complicated design theory

How This Guide Will Help

By the end of this guide, you will:

  • understand the real purpose of a website
  • identify the feeling your website should create
  • improve visitor experience
  • create a more welcoming online presence
  • make better design decisions

Core Observation

People do not visit websites.

They enter spaces.

Think about visiting:

  • a cozy café
  • a crowded market
  • a quiet library
  • a luxury hotel

Each space creates a feeling.

Websites do the same thing.

Every design choice communicates something.

Every page creates an experience.

The question is whether that experience is intentional.

Main Framework

Step 1: Decide How You Want People To Feel

Before choosing:

  • colors
  • fonts
  • images
  • layouts

Ask:

“When someone arrives, what should they feel?”

Examples:

A writer may want visitors to feel:

  • curious
  • reflective
  • calm

A coach may want visitors to feel:

  • hopeful
  • supported
  • confident

A designer may want visitors to feel:

  • inspired
  • excited
  • creative

Choose the feeling first.

Build everything else around it.

Step 2: Remove Friction

Many websites accidentally create stress.

Examples:

  • too many menu options
  • cluttered layouts
  • confusing language
  • competing calls-to-action

Visitors should not have to work hard.

Make the next step obvious.

Step 3: Guide The Visitor

Every page should answer:

“What should this person do next?”

Examples:

  • read an article
  • view a portfolio
  • send an email
  • download a guide

Do not leave visitors wandering.

Guide them gently.

Step 4: Speak Like A Human

Many websites sound like corporate reports.

People connect with people.

Use language that feels:

  • clear
  • natural
  • direct

Imagine explaining your work to a thoughtful friend.

Write that way.

Step 5: Create Consistency

A digital home should feel coherent.

Your:

  • words
  • colors
  • images
  • tone

should feel like they belong together.

Consistency creates trust.

Field Observations

Observation #1

People often spend more time choosing colors than clarifying their message.

The message matters more.

Observation #2

Simple websites are often easier to trust.

Observation #3

Visitors usually arrive with one question:

“Am I in the right place?”

Your homepage should answer that immediately.

Observation #4

A website that feels clear often performs better than a website that looks impressive.

Observation #5

People remember experiences long after they forget features.

Practical Exercise

Open your homepage.

Imagine you have never seen it before.

Ask:

  • What feeling does this create?
  • Is that intentional?
  • What feels confusing?
  • What feels welcoming?
  • What would I remove?

Write down your answers.

Then choose one improvement.

Implement it today.

Quick Start Version

Today:

  1. Choose three words that describe how visitors should feel.
  2. Review your homepage.
  3. Remove one unnecessary element.
  4. Make one next step more obvious.

That’s enough.

Checklist

Ask yourself:

  • Do visitors know what I do?
  • Do visitors know who this is for?
  • Do visitors know what to do next?
  • Does the website create the feeling I want?
  • Is anything creating unnecessary confusion?

If not, simplify.

Next Step

Choose one page on your website.

Not the entire website.

One page.

Improve:

  • clarity
  • feeling
  • guidance

Then stop.

Small improvements compound.

Final Reflection

A brochure distributes information.

A home creates an experience.

People may forget what your website said.

They rarely forget how it made them feel.

Build accordingly.

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative identity of Rakshitha Prakash—an outlet that reflects her exploration of creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts and restarts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

What part landed hardest?

What are you struggling with right now?

If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya

entry.title: The Digital Cottage Field Guide
entry.summary: Digital Cottage Build a Calm Online Presence Introduction Much of the internet feels like a city that never sleeps. Notifications. Updates. Algorithms. New platforms. New tools. New things to maintain. Many creators unintentionally build themselves a digital mansion they must constantly clean, repair, update, and…
entry.content:
Digital Cottage

Build a Calm Online Presence

Introduction

Much of the internet feels like a city that never sleeps.

Notifications. Updates. Algorithms. New platforms. New tools. New things to maintain.

Many creators unintentionally build themselves a digital mansion they must constantly clean, repair, update, and defend.

Then they wonder why they feel exhausted.

The problem is often not the creator.

The problem is the architecture.

This guide introduces a different approach:

The Digital Cottage.

A small, intentional online presence designed to support your work instead of consuming your life.

Who This Is For

This guide is for:

  • writers
  • artists
  • creators
  • freelancers
  • solopreneurs
  • people who feel overwhelmed by technology
  • people who want a simple online presence
  • people who value sustainability over speed

Who This Is Not For

This guide is not for:

  • people building large ecommerce businesses
  • people running complex membership sites
  • people managing large teams
  • people who enjoy constantly optimizing technology

How This Guide Will Help

By the end of this guide, you will:

  • understand the Digital Cottage approach
  • simplify your online presence
  • choose tools more intentionally
  • reduce maintenance
  • create a calmer publishing system

Core Observation

Most creators do not need more tools.

They need fewer responsibilities.

Every tool creates work.

Every platform creates maintenance.

Every system creates decisions.

The goal is not to build the biggest online presence.

The goal is to build one you can actually sustain.

Main Framework

Step 1: Build Only What You Need Today

Ask:

“What problem am I solving right now?”

Examples:

If you need:

  • a website

Build:

  • a website

Do not also build:

  • a membership area
  • a course platform
  • a community forum
  • a podcast network

Build today’s solution.

Not next year’s fantasy.

Step 2: Count Maintenance Costs

Every tool costs something.

Not always money.

Sometimes:

  • time
  • attention
  • learning
  • troubleshooting
  • updates

Before adding a tool, ask:

“What will this require from me six months from now?”

If the answer feels exhausting, reconsider.

Step 3: Choose Calm Tools

Look for tools that are:

  • simple
  • reliable
  • understandable
  • easy to replace

Avoid tools that require constant monitoring.

A good tool should disappear into the background.

Step 4: Publish More, Manage Less

Your audience cares about:

  • your ideas
  • your work
  • your solutions

They rarely care about your technology stack.

Spend more time creating.

Spend less time maintaining.

Step 5: Leave Room For Real Life

Life happens.

People get sick.

Families need attention.

Energy changes.

A Digital Cottage survives interruptions.

It can be left alone for a week and still be standing when you return.

Field Observations

Observation #1

Most creators overbuild.

Very few underbuild.

Observation #2

The simplest system you can maintain will often outperform the complex system you abandon.

Observation #3

Many people are trying to scale before they have something worth scaling.

Observation #4

Technology should support creativity.

Not replace it.

Observation #5

A calm creator often produces more meaningful work than an overwhelmed creator.

Practical Exercise

Make a list of every tool you currently use.

For each tool ask:

  • What problem does this solve?
  • How often do I actually use it?
  • What maintenance does it require?
  • Would I add it again today?

Highlight anything that no longer earns its place.

Remove one unnecessary tool this week.

Quick Start Version

Today:

  1. List your tools.
  2. Remove one unnecessary tool.
  3. Identify your most important platform.
  4. Spend one hour improving that platform instead of adding a new one.

Checklist

Before adding a new tool:

  • Does it solve a real problem?
  • Do I need it now?
  • Can I maintain it?
  • Does it simplify or complicate my life?
  • Would I still choose it six months from now?

If you cannot answer yes to most of these questions, wait.

Next Step

Choose one area of your online presence.

Examples:

  • website
  • newsletter
  • social media
  • publishing workflow

Simplify it.

Do not add anything.

Remove something.

See what becomes easier.

Final Reflection

Many people dream of building digital empires.

Few ask whether they want to live there.

A Digital Cottage is smaller.

Quieter.

Simpler.

And for many creators, it is exactly the right size.

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative identity of Rakshitha Prakash—an outlet that reflects her exploration of creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts and restarts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

What part landed hardest?

What are you struggling with right now?

If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya

entry.title: The Build Things That Last Field Guide
entry.summary: Build Things That Last Why Some Work Disappears and Some Keeps Growing Some work disappears quickly. Some work keeps helping people for years. The difference is not always quality. Often, the difference is where the work lives and how it is built. A social media…
entry.content:
Build Things That Last

Why Some Work Disappears and Some Keeps Growing

Introduction

Some work disappears quickly.

Some work keeps helping people for years.

The difference is not always quality.

Often, the difference is where the work lives and how it is built.

A social media post may be seen by hundreds of people today and almost nobody next month.

A useful guide may continue helping people for years.

A trending video may disappear.

A library grows.

This Field Guide explores that difference.

Not to argue against attention.

But to understand how to build work that remains useful long after it is created.

Who This Is For

This guide is for:

  • creators
  • writers
  • artists
  • educators
  • coaches
  • solopreneurs
  • people building a body of work

It is especially useful for people who feel like they are constantly creating but have very little to show for it.

Who This Is Not For

This guide is probably not for:

  • people seeking rapid audience growth tactics
  • people focused entirely on short-term marketing campaigns
  • teams building large-scale advertising systems

This guide focuses on long-term accumulation.

How This Guide Will Help

After reading this guide, readers will:

  • understand why some work compounds and some disappears
  • identify assets they already own
  • make better decisions about where to invest effort
  • build resources people can return to
  • create a simple plan for building lasting value

The Core Observation

Many creators spend years creating things that disappear.

Not because the work is bad.

Not because the ideas are weak.

Not because they lack skill.

The work disappears because it was designed for attention instead of accumulation.

Attention creates a moment.

Assets create a library.

Understanding that difference changes how work gets built.

The Difference Between Attention And Assets

Attention creates visibility.

Assets create value that remains available.

Examples of attention:

  • likes
  • views
  • impressions
  • shares
  • comments

Examples of assets:

  • websites
  • articles
  • guides
  • publications
  • email lists
  • templates
  • resource libraries

Attention moves.

Assets accumulate.

Why Attention Feels More Rewarding

Attention produces immediate feedback.

A notification appears.

A comment arrives.

A number increases.

The response is visible.

Assets work differently.

A guide may take days to create.

A resource page may take weeks.

An archive may take months.

The reward is delayed.

Because of that delay, many people abandon asset-building too early.

What Lasting Work Looks Like

Lasting work usually has three characteristics.

It Can Be Found Again

People can return to it.

Examples:

  • websites
  • articles
  • guides

It Can Be Improved

The work does not disappear after publishing.

It can be updated.

Expanded.

Refined.

It Continues Helping

The creator does not need to recreate it every week.

The work remains available.

The Library Test

Imagine someone discovers your work today.

Then asks:

“What else is here?”

What would they find?

Possibilities:

  • a collection of useful articles
  • guides
  • publications
  • resources
  • templates

Or:

  • a social media profile with old posts buried beneath newer ones

The answer reveals whether work is accumulating.

Common Assets Worth Building

Not every asset needs to be large.

Useful examples include:

Articles

One useful idea.

One useful solution.

Guides

Focused practical resources.

Publications

Longer collections of knowledge.

Resource Libraries

Curated collections of useful material.

Email Lists

A direct connection with readers.

Templates

Tools people can reuse.

Websites

A home for everything else.

Field Observations

The following patterns appear repeatedly.

Most People Already Have Assets

Old articles.

Old PDFs.

Old newsletters.

Old resources.

Old notes.

Old checklists.

Many assets already exist.

They simply have not been organized or recognized yet.

Asset-Building Feels Slower Than It Is

A guide may sit quietly for months.

Then someone discovers it.

Then shares it.

Then another person finds it.

Many assets grow gradually.

Not dramatically.

Attention Is Easier To Measure

Views are visible.

Likes are visible.

Comments are visible.

Assets often grow quietly.

This causes many people to overvalue attention and undervalue accumulation.

The Same Work Can Become An Asset

A social media post can become:

  • an article
  • a guide
  • a checklist
  • a publication

Many assets begin as small pieces of content.

The Asset Audit

Take ten minutes.

List everything that currently exists.

Examples:

  • articles
  • blog posts
  • PDFs
  • newsletters
  • email subscribers
  • templates
  • checklists
  • guides
  • resource collections
  • website pages

Next to each item, mark:

  • Asset
  • Attention
  • Both

Now ask:

Which assets need improvement?

Which attention pieces could become assets?

The answers usually reveal the next useful project.

Quick Start Version

Choose one recent piece of content.

Ask:

How can this become an asset?

Examples:

Instead of:

  • a social media post

Create:

  • an article

Instead of:

  • answering the same question repeatedly

Create:

  • a guide

Instead of:

  • sharing scattered resources

Create:

  • a resource page

Choose one.

Complete it today.

Common Mistakes

Mistake One

Treating attention as the final goal.

Attention is useful.

It is rarely the destination.

Mistake Two

Waiting until everything is perfect before creating assets.

Small assets count.

A one-page guide is still an asset.

Mistake Three

Building only for platforms.

Platforms can help people discover the work.

They should not be the only place the work exists.

Mistake Four

Creating New Things Instead Of Improving Existing Assets

Many assets become more valuable through maintenance.

Not replacement.

Checklist

Before creating something, ask:

  • Will this still be useful next month?
  • Can people find it later?
  • Can it be improved?
  • Can it be reused?
  • Does it belong somewhere I control?
  • Does it add to a growing library?

The more yes answers, the more likely it is to become a lasting asset.

Next Step

Complete the Asset Audit.

Choose one existing piece of work.

Turn it into a lasting asset.

Not five pieces.

Not ten.

One.

Finish it.

Then choose the next one.

Final Reflection

A post may be forgotten tomorrow.

A guide may still be helping someone years from now.

Both take time to create.

Only one continues working after the creator has moved on.

That’s the difference worth noticing.

entry.title: The Publishing as Market Research Field Guide
entry.summary: Publishing as Market Research How To Discover What People Want By Publishing Useful Things Many people treat publishing as the final step. The guide gets written. The product gets built. The website goes live. The launch begins. Only then does feedback arrive. Sometimes the feedback…
entry.content:
Publishing as Market Research

How To Discover What People Want By Publishing Useful Things

Many people treat publishing as the final step.

The guide gets written.

The product gets built.

The website goes live.

The launch begins.

Only then does feedback arrive.

Sometimes the feedback is encouraging.

Sometimes it reveals a problem.

The wrong topic.

The wrong audience.

The wrong solution.

The wrong assumptions.

That can be an expensive way to learn.

There is a simpler approach.

Use publishing as market research.

The Traditional Approach

The traditional approach often looks like this:

Research.

Plan.

Build.

Launch.

Hope.

The challenge is obvious.

Months of work can be invested before discovering whether anyone actually cares.

A Different Approach

Instead:

Publish.

Learn.

Adapt.

Repeat.

Small publications create information.

That information becomes more valuable than assumptions.

What Counts As Publishing?

Publishing does not require:

  • a large audience
  • a website
  • a newsletter
  • a course
  • a business

Publishing simply means sharing something useful.

Examples:

  • A Field Guide
  • A checklist
  • A worksheet
  • A framework
  • A resource list
  • A short article

The goal is not scale.

The goal is learning.

Every Publication Answers Questions

A useful publication can reveal:

  • what people care about
  • what people ignore
  • what people misunderstand
  • what people struggle with
  • what people want more of

That information is difficult to discover in theory.

It becomes obvious in practice.

The BE → DO → HAVE Example

Imagine a short guide is published about BE → DO → HAVE.

Some readers enjoy it.

Some readers ignore it.

Some readers reply.

A pattern appears.

Many responses mention the same thing:

“I know what to do. I just don’t do it.”

That is valuable information.

A stronger publication idea has just appeared.

Not because of brainstorming.

Because readers revealed it.

Small Publications Create Fast Feedback

Large projects create slow feedback.

Small projects create fast feedback.

A three-page guide can teach more than three months of planning.

Not because it is better.

Because it reaches reality sooner.

Reality is an excellent teacher.

Questions Are More Valuable Than Praise

Many creators look for approval.

A more useful goal is understanding.

Instead of asking:

“Did you like it?”

Ask:

  • What part landed hardest?
  • What are you struggling with right now?
  • What would you like more help with?

These questions create better information.

Patterns Matter

One response is interesting.

Five responses are encouraging.

Twenty responses become useful.

Look for repetition.

Repeated questions.

Repeated frustrations.

Repeated language.

Repeated desires.

Patterns reveal opportunities.

Publishing From Zero Audience

This approach works particularly well for people starting from scratch.

No audience?

No problem.

Start small.

Share with:

  • friends
  • colleagues
  • communities
  • existing contacts

The goal is not reach.

The goal is response.

Ten thoughtful readers can teach more than a thousand passive followers.

The Publishing Research Loop

Step 1:

Create something useful.

Step 2:

Publish it.

Step 3:

Ask questions.

Step 4:

Collect responses.

Step 5:

Look for patterns.

Step 6:

Create the next publication based on what was learned.

Then repeat.

Why This Works

Most people try to discover demand before creating anything.

Publishing creates evidence.

Evidence reduces guessing.

Less guessing leads to better decisions.

Better decisions create more useful work.

The First Experiment

Choose one idea.

Create the smallest useful version.

Publish it.

Ask questions.

Collect responses.

Study patterns.

Then decide what to build next.

That is enough.

Reflection Questions

  • What publication could be created this week?
  • What assumptions currently exist about readers?
  • What information would be most useful right now?
  • What could be learned by publishing instead of planning?

Final Thought

Publishing is not just a way to share ideas.

It is a way to discover them.

Every useful publication teaches something.

The question is not:

“What should be built next?”

The question is:

“What is the next thing reality can teach?”

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative practice of Rakshitha Prakash—a space for exploring creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

  1. What part landed hardest?

  2. What are you struggling with right now?

  3. If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya

entry.title: The Identity Selection Method Field Guide
entry.summary: The Identity Selection Method How To Choose An Identity That Actually Changes Something Many people begin identity work with a question like: Who do I want to be? It sounds useful. But it often creates confusion. The answers tend to be vague. The problem is…
entry.content:
The Identity Selection Method

How To Choose An Identity That Actually Changes Something

Many people begin identity work with a question like:

Who do I want to be?

It sounds useful.

But it often creates confusion.

The answers tend to be vague.

  • Confident
  • Successful
  • Wealthy
  • Productive
  • Aligned

The problem is that these identities are difficult to use.

What does “be confident” actually look like on a Tuesday afternoon?

What does “be successful” actually look like today?

A more useful question is:

What identity would naturally create the outcome I’m looking for?

Why Most Identity Work Fails

Many identities sound good.

Few are practical.

Examples:

“I am abundant.”

“I am successful.”

“I am powerful.”

Perhaps.

But what happens next?

What action follows?

What changes today?

If an identity doesn’t influence behaviour, it is difficult to build.

The Observable Identity Rule

A simple rule:

Choose identities that can be observed.

Someone watching your actions should be able to recognise the identity.

Examples:

Instead of:

“I am creative.”

Try:

“I am someone who creates.”

Instead of:

“I am healthy.”

Try:

“I am someone who moves my body daily.”

Instead of:

“I am organised.”

Try:

“I am someone who puts things away after using them.”

The more observable the identity, the easier it becomes to practice.

Start With The Outcome

Most people already know what they want.

Examples:

  • Write a book
  • Build a YouTube channel
  • Declutter a home
  • Improve fitness
  • Create a body of work

Start there.

Write down the outcome.

Then ask:

Who naturally creates this result?

Work Backwards

Example:

Outcome:

Publish useful resources.

Question:

Who naturally publishes useful resources?

Possible identity:

I am someone who ships useful work consistently.

Now the path becomes clearer.

Example One: Wild Womaniya

Desired outcome:

Create practical resources that people can actually use.

Possible identity:

I am someone who experiments, documents, and publishes.

Natural actions:

  • Write Field Guides.
  • Test ideas.
  • Share observations.
  • Publish consistently.

Notice what happened.

The identity creates the actions.

The actions create the outcome.

Example Two: The Ecstatic Alchemist

Desired outcome:

Help people close the gap between knowing and doing.

Possible identity:

I am someone who practices what I teach.

Natural actions:

  • Run small experiments.
  • Test ideas personally.
  • Document observations.
  • Share practical lessons.

Again, the identity points toward behaviour.

Example Three: Decluttering

Desired outcome:

A calmer, less cluttered home.

Possible identity:

I am someone who makes small decluttering decisions daily.

Natural actions:

  • Remove one item.
  • Repair something.
  • Put things away.
  • Donate unused items.

The identity makes daily decisions easier.

Avoid Fantasy Identities

Some identities feel exciting.

But they are too far away from present reality.

Examples:

  • Bestselling author
  • Millionaire entrepreneur
  • World-class expert

These are outcomes disguised as identities.

They don’t tell you what to do today.

Focus on identities that can be practiced immediately.

Choose The Smallest Useful Identity

Smaller identities are easier to build.

Instead of:

“I am a successful creator.”

Try:

“I am someone who publishes.”

Instead of:

“I am financially free.”

Try:

“I am someone who manages money carefully.”

Small identities survive real life.

The Identity Test

Before choosing an identity, ask:

  1. Is it observable?

  2. Does it suggest clear actions?

  3. Can it be practiced today?

  4. Would repeating those actions create the outcome I want?

If the answer is yes, the identity is probably useful.

The Identity Selection Exercise

Step 1

Write down one outcome you want.

Step 2

Ask:

Who naturally creates this outcome?

Step 3

Turn the answer into an identity.

I am someone who:

Step 4

List three actions this person naturally does.

Step 5

Choose one action to practice this week.

Reflection Questions

  • Am I choosing identities or outcomes?
  • Can my identity be observed?
  • What action naturally follows from this identity?
  • What identity would make my next goal easier?

Final Thought

The goal is not to choose the most impressive identity.

The goal is to choose the most useful one.

A useful identity changes behaviour.

Changed behaviour creates different results.

Start with the outcome.

Work backwards.

Choose the identity.

Practice the actions.

Let the results follow.

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative practice of Rakshitha Prakash—a space for exploring creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

  1. What part landed hardest?

  2. What are you struggling with right now?

  3. If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya

entry.title: The 'One Problem, One Method, One Audience' Field Guide
entry.summary: One Problem, One Method, One Audience Why Audience Usually Comes Last One of the most common pieces of advice for creators is: “Find your audience.” Sounds reasonable. Yet many people get stuck there. Questions appear immediately: Hours disappear. Days disappear. Sometimes months disappear. Meanwhile, nothing…
entry.content:
One Problem, One Method, One Audience

Why Audience Usually Comes Last

One of the most common pieces of advice for creators is:

“Find your audience.”

Sounds reasonable.

Yet many people get stuck there.

Questions appear immediately:

  • Who are they?
  • How old are they?
  • What do they do?
  • What are their interests?
  • What do they want?

Hours disappear.

Days disappear.

Sometimes months disappear.

Meanwhile, nothing gets created.

A simpler approach is:

Start with a problem.

Develop a method.

Discover the audience.

Start With A Problem

Most useful work begins with a problem.

Something frustrating.

Something recurring.

Something difficult to ignore.

A question that keeps appearing.

A challenge that keeps showing up.

The problem becomes the starting point.

Not demographics.

Not audience avatars.

Not market segments.

A real problem.

Then Develop A Method

Once a problem is visible, experimentation begins.

Different approaches get tested.

Some work.

Some don’t.

Patterns start appearing.

Eventually a method emerges.

Not because it was planned from the beginning.

Because repeated experiments revealed what worked.

The method becomes the bridge between the problem and the solution.

Then Discover The Audience

Once the work is shared, something interesting happens.

People start responding.

Questions appear.

Emails arrive.

Conversations begin.

Certain people recognise themselves in the problem.

Those people become the audience.

The audience was not invented.

It was discovered.

Example One: Wild Womaniya

The starting point was not an audience.

The starting point was an observation.

Many people want practical change.

Many resources provide inspiration.

Far fewer provide implementation.

So the experiment became:

What happens when practical resources become the focus?

Field Guides.

Handbooks.

Experiments.

Simple systems.

The audience appeared afterwards.

People who enjoy learning by doing started paying attention.

People who prefer practical action over endless inspiration started paying attention.

The work attracted them.

Example Two: The Ecstatic Alchemist

The starting point was a question.

Why do people know what to do and still struggle to do it?

That question appeared again and again.

A method slowly emerged.

BE.

DO.

HAVE.

Identity shifts.

Small experiments.

Observable actions.

The audience appeared later.

People caught between knowing and doing recognised the problem immediately.

Many of them were interested in personal growth.

Many were interested in spirituality.

Many were simply tired of staying stuck.

The audience emerged from the problem.

Example Three: Marie Kondo

The starting point was clutter.

Homes felt crowded.

Possessions felt overwhelming.

Decision-making felt exhausting.

A method emerged:

  • Keep what sparks joy.
  • Tidy by category.
  • Finish completely.

People struggling with clutter recognised themselves in the problem.

The audience followed naturally.

Why This Matters

Starting with an audience often leads to guessing.

Starting with a problem leads to observation.

Observation leads to experimentation.

Experimentation leads to methods.

Useful methods attract people.

Those people become the audience.

A Simpler Way To Begin

Instead of asking:

Who is my audience?

Try asking:

What problem keeps showing up?

Then ask:

What am I testing?

Then ask:

Who seems interested whenever this topic comes up?

That is often enough to begin.

The One Problem Exercise

Step 1

Write down one problem that keeps appearing.

Not ten.

One.

Step 2

Write down what has already been tested.

Step 3

Write down what seems to be working.

Step 4

Write down who seems most interested.

Reflection Questions

  • What problem keeps showing up?
  • What experiments have already been run?
  • What method is beginning to emerge?
  • Who seems interested in the results?

Final Thought

Many people wait until they have identified the perfect audience.

Useful work rarely begins that way.

A problem appears.

A method emerges.

The work gets shared.

The audience reveals itself.

One problem.

One method.

One audience.

In that order.

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative practice of Rakshitha Prakash—a space for exploring creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

  1. What part landed hardest?

  2. What are you struggling with right now?

  3. If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya

entry.title: The "I Know Everything. Why Am I Not Doing Anything?" Field Guide
entry.summary: I Know Everything. Why Am I Not Doing Anything? Escaping The Consumption Trap There is a particular kind of frustration that appears after years of personal growth work. You have read the books. Listened to the podcasts. Taken the courses. Watched the videos. Filled notebooks…
entry.content:
I Know Everything. Why Am I Not Doing Anything?

Escaping The Consumption Trap

There is a particular kind of frustration that appears after years of personal growth work.

You have read the books.

Listened to the podcasts.

Taken the courses.

Watched the videos.

Filled notebooks with ideas.

You know more than you knew five years ago.

Possibly more than most people around you.

And yet:

The thing you want to do remains undone.

The project remains unfinished.

The habit never quite sticks.

The change never fully arrives.

At some point, a difficult question appears:

Why am I still stuck?

The Knowledge Problem

Most people assume the solution is more information.

Another book.

Another framework.

Another expert.

Another breakthrough.

Sometimes information is genuinely missing.

But often, it isn’t.

Many people already know enough to take the next step.

The issue is not a lack of knowledge.

The issue is the gap between knowing and doing.

The Consumption Trap

The trap looks productive.

You are learning.

Researching.

Exploring.

Taking notes.

Highlighting ideas.

Thinking deeply.

Everything feels useful.

And much of it is.

Until learning becomes a substitute for action.

That is when the trap begins.

Why Learning Feels So Good

Learning creates momentum without risk.

You feel progress.

Without exposure.

Without uncertainty.

Without failure.

Without judgment.

You can spend hours learning.

And never discover whether the idea works in real life.

Action is different.

Action produces feedback.

Feedback can be uncomfortable.

Which is why many people stay in learning mode.

The Hidden Forms Of Avoidance

Avoidance rarely announces itself.

It often disguises itself as something responsible.

Examples:

“I need more clarity first.”

“I need to research a little more.”

“I need the right system.”

“I need to feel aligned.”

“I’ll start next week.”

Each statement sounds reasonable.

Collectively, they can delay action for years.

The Cost Of Staying Here

The biggest cost is not time.

It is self-trust.

Every time you promise yourself something and do not follow through, a small crack appears.

Over time, those cracks accumulate.

You begin doubting yourself.

Not because you lack ability.

Because you have trained yourself not to believe your own commitments.

The Shift

The solution is surprisingly small.

Stop asking:

“What else do I need to learn?”

Start asking:

“What am I willing to practice?”

Knowledge changes very little.

Practice changes people.

One Thing

Choose one thing.

Not ten.

Not an entire life overhaul.

One thing.

One action.

One commitment.

Examples:

  • Write for ten minutes.
  • Walk for fifteen minutes.
  • Publish one piece of content.
  • Declutter one item.

Small actions are easier to repeat.

Repeated actions create change.

Action Creates Clarity

Many people wait for clarity before acting.

In reality, clarity often follows action.

You learn:

  • what works
  • what doesn’t
  • what feels natural
  • what needs adjustment

Not through thinking.

Through doing.

A Better Relationship With Growth

Growth does not require constant consumption.

Sometimes growth looks like:

Reading less.

Practicing more.

Planning less.

Doing more.

Collecting fewer ideas.

Implementing one.

The goal is not to know everything.

The goal is to become someone who uses what they know.

The Seven-Day Experiment

For the next seven days:

Choose one thing you already know would help.

Not something new.

Something familiar.

Something you’ve been postponing.

Commit to doing it every day.

No additional research.

No optimization.

No new systems.

Just practice.

Observe what happens.

Reflection Questions

  • What have I been learning instead of doing?
  • What action have I been postponing?
  • What am I hoping another book or course will solve for me?
  • What would happen if I practiced instead of prepared?

Final Thought

You probably do not need another breakthrough.

You probably need a repetition.

Not another idea.

Another action.

The gap between knowing and doing is rarely closed by information.

It is closed by practice.

About The Ecstatic Alchemist

The Ecstatic Alchemist is the creative practice of Rakshitha Prakash—a space for exploring creativity, personal growth, simple living, and taking small steps toward meaningful change.

At its heart is a simple question:

What becomes possible when we stop waiting and start doing?

About Wild Womaniya

Wild Womaniya creates practical guides, resources, and experiments for people who learn by doing.

The goal isn’t perfection.

It’s progress.

Everything is designed to be useful in real life—with all its messiness, interruptions, and fresh starts.

Continue the Conversation

If something in this guide resonated with you, I’d love to hear from you.

Email:

wildwomaniyastudio@gmail.com

If you’d like to write back, here are three questions that would help me most:

  1. What part landed hardest?

  2. What are you struggling with right now?

  3. If I expanded one section into a full handbook, which section should it be?

— The Ecstatic Alchemist

Wild Womaniya
